notes:
Plaintiff opening work notes, arb-ex05-20260630003505.

Opportunity: openings:plaintiff. wait_for_opportunity returned ready; allowed final action record_opening_statement only. Text limit 5000; no evidence submission or technical reports allowed.

Record/evidence inspection: get_case showed empty record: no evidence, exhibits, submitted evidence, arguments, openings, rebuttals, surrebuttals, technical_reports, or closings. list_evidence returned empty evidence list; therefore no case-packet file or admitted source exists to stat/read. No record evidence can be cited yet.

Issue map: Plaintiff must prove by preponderance and consensus of credible sources that between 2025-11-03 and 2026-01-31 23:59 ET the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory. Venezuela/U.S. de facto-controlled land as of 2025-09-06 counts as sovereign territory. Decisive elements are: qualifying date, U.S. military conduct, Venezuelan territory, offensive character, and intent to establish control over any portion, including temporary/tactical control if source support later establishes that.

Native investigation/source map: Web searches run: (1) United States invasion Venezuela November 2025 January 2026 military offensive control territory; (2) US military offensive Venezuela November 2025 January 2026 invasion; (3) Venezuela U.S. troops invasion November 2025 January 2026 credible sources; (4) United States commenced military offensive Venezuela December 2025. Leads found, not yet admitted evidence: War.gov Jan. 3, 2026 Pentagon News official announcement re U.S. military extraction/capture in Caracas; PBS/AP timeline with Dec. 16 blockade, Dec. 30 first known direct operation on Venezuelan soil, Jan. 3 large-scale strike across Caracas and capture/removal of Maduro/Flores; AP timeline with force buildup, CIA authorization, blockade, contested legality, Jan. 3 strike; CFR explainer re Operation Southern Spear/buildup; Brookings analysis re Jan. 3 special operation to exfiltrate Maduro and wife from Caracas.

Working theory: Jan. 3, 2026 is the likely clearest qualifying event if later admitted evidence shows U.S. forces used military strikes/raid forces in Caracas, suppressed defenses or controlled operational areas, seized Maduro/Flores, and removed them. December 2025 blockade, tanker seizures, CIA/covert action, force posture, and statements are context showing the campaign and intent, but may be weaker standing alone because sea actions or unattributed covert action may not establish land control.

Adverse checks/limitations: Defense will likely call the operation law-enforcement extraction against narcoterrorism, not invasion or territorial-control offensive. U.S. official source wording may avoid invasion and emphasize capture/extraction. Some sources are secondary; several relevant details may be classified, behind article links, or require exact capture later. Need source-chain preservation in evidence phase before relying on any URLs or article facts.

Private durable journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md with source URLs, search log, theory, adverse issues, and unresolved gaps.

Draft filing approach: Opening will be forward-looking, candid that the record is empty, and will focus the council on the real dispute: whether credible-source consensus later proves an armed U.S. campaign culminating in Jan. 3 Caracas control/extraction, versus a narrow law-enforcement operation. It will identify proof needed and what would defeat plaintiff's burden.

Opening opportunity openings:defendant. Record checked with get_case: only plaintiff opening; no admitted evidence, exhibits, submitted evidence, technical reports, arguments, rebuttals, closings. list_evidence returned empty list. Opening rules do not permit evidence submission. Source map: decisive elements are timing, U.S. military offensive, intent to establish control over any portion of Venezuela, and credible-source consensus under preponderance. Outside investigation performed through web search with queries: (1) United States invaded Venezuela January 2026 Caracas operation Maduro extraction; (2) January 2026 U.S. military operation Caracas Venezuela Maduro credible sources; (3) site:whitehouse.gov Venezuela January 2026 Caracas operation United States military; (4) site:defense.gov Venezuela January 2026 U.S. forces Caracas. Leads located but not admitted: Defense.gov Jan. 3, 2026 article on U.S. military capture/extraction of Maduro in Caracas; media.defense.gov Operation Southern Spear report PDF; Brookings Jan. 2026 analysis; CSIS imagery analysis framing strikes as surgical; Wikipedia/source-map aggregation. Defense theory: plaintiff currently has no admitted proof; even if later sources prove a raid/extraction/capture and strikes, plaintiff must prove the qualifying fact: an offensive intended to establish territorial control, not merely temporary tactical access, airspace denial, perimeter security, force protection, or law-enforcement seizure of a person. Need later evidence phase to capture official statements, report PDF, independent reporting, imagery analysis, court docket materials, and adverse/limiting sources. Unresolved gaps: exact admitted text; source-chain/date ambiguity Jan. 2 vs Jan. 3; whether any source establishes control as objective rather than incidental capability; whether there was consent/cooperation by Venezuelan authorities or later transition actors. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.

Plaintiff merits argument work notes, arb-ex05-20260630003505.

Opportunity: arguments:plaintiff. Allowed final filing: submit_argument. Evidence submission allowed. Limits: 6000 chars for argument; up to 9 exhibits; plaintiff had 0 submitted evidence at start.

Record/evidence scan: get_case showed plaintiff and defendant openings only; no evidence at phase start. list_evidence initially empty. After submissions, list_evidence confirmed three plaintiff submitted evidence items visible with hashes.

Evidence plan: Submit compact faithful text captures preserving source URL, retrieval timestamp, relevance, and limitations. Focus on (1) official U.S. admission, (2) independent AP/PBS timeline corroboration and sequence, (3) CFR credible analysis for Operation Southern Spear context and adverse counternarcotics framing.

Evidence accepted:
PX-1 planned label: ev_e0e0fb921ec9_submitted-evidence-01-plaintiff-e0e0fb921ec9. Title: Official U.S. Department of War account of Operation Absolute Resolve in Caracas. SHA-256 e0e0fb921ec90639876fb9c4c5697b4f6e3a0fd6a37b4b6befc970412f1f4687, 3556 bytes. Key content: U.S. military extraction in Caracas; Trump says U.S. armed forces conducted extraordinary military operation in Venezuela's capital against heavily fortified military fortress in heart of Caracas; months of planning and rehearsal involving joint forces and special operations; integrated air/ground/space/maritime operations; 150+ aircraft provided cover for ground extraction force in Caracas; Hegseth called it a massive joint military and law enforcement raid. Weight: official U.S. admission; adverse caveat: law-enforcement/criminal charges framing.

PX-2 planned label: ev_7d30fcd0d9f7_submitted-evidence-02-plaintiff-7d30fcd0d9f7. Title: PBS/AP timeline of U.S. escalation leading to Maduro capture. SHA-256 7d30fcd0d9f704222f94166d25b9a1221e206e971b247a0beb4c9cee7ba25372, 3389 bytes. Key content: Dec. 10 tanker seizure; Dec. 16 blockade; Dec. 30 first known direct operation on Venezuelan soil (anonymous-source and non-acknowledgement caveats); Jan. 3 large-scale strike across Caracas, capture of Maduro/Flores, flown out of country. Weight: independent chronology; strong corroboration; limitations preserved.

PX-3 planned label: ev_051e811bed13_submitted-evidence-03-plaintiff-051e811bed13. Title: CFR explainer on Operation Southern Spear and Venezuela. SHA-256 051e811bed130472bf2072ff5316764473d68daa0df391ba0a3dd5da10dc88f0, 4001 bytes. Key content: Operation Southern Spear, expanded air/naval presence, formal military campaign, more than twenty lethal strikes, blockade, expert view that scope exceeded counternarcotics and raised regime-change possibility, approx. 15,000 personnel, practical need for physical control of streets. Weight: credible secondary context, not primary proof of specific Jan. 3 details; useful for scale/intent/adverse framing.

Argument theory: The qualifying event is Jan. 3, 2026. PX-1 official source and PX-2 independent AP/PBS account establish military force inside Caracas, a ground extraction force, a heavily fortified military objective, large air support, capture/removal of Maduro/Flores. PX-3 shows this was not isolated police work but part of a formal U.S. military campaign and regional buildup. Control element: the proposition says 'any portion,' not occupation or permanent sovereignty; the operation required control over the fortress/compound/objective area and air-ground corridor at least temporarily. Consensus: the official and independent sources converge on armed U.S. operation in Caracas; source differences concern legal framing and rationale, not the core operational facts.

Adverse/limitations addressed in filing: law enforcement and counternarcotics framing; Dec. 30 anonymous sourcing; no need to prove permanent occupation; naval blockade alone would not be enough. Missing items not submitted this phase: full HTML captures/media files, satellite imagery, primary transcript, DOJ indictment, Venezuelan/UN statements. Rebuttal should target defendant's 'hold territory' theory and potentially add primary transcript/imagery/Venezuelan statements if needed.

Private journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.

Merits opportunity arguments:defendant. Checked current record and evidence list. Plaintiff added three submitted evidence extracts: PX-1 official Department of War/Pentagon News Operation Absolute Resolve article (ev_e0e0fb921ec9..., sha256 e0e0fb921ec90639876fb9c4c5697b4f6e3a0fd6a37b4b6befc970412f1f4687, 3556 bytes); PX-2 PBS/AP timeline (ev_7d30fcd0d9f7..., sha256 7d30fcd0d9f704222f94166d25b9a1221e206e971b247a0beb4c9cee7ba25372, 3389 bytes); PX-3 CFR explainer (ev_051e811bed13..., sha256 051e811bed130472bf2072ff5316764473d68daa0df391ba0a3dd5da10dc88f0, 4001 bytes). Used stat_evidence and read_evidence_range for all three. Analysis: plaintiff evidence is strong on U.S. military force in Caracas within the window, and on capture/removal of Maduro/Flores. It is weak on whether the intended object was territorial control. PX-1 states mission was to bring Maduro to justice and calls it joint military/law-enforcement. PX-2 contains caveats for Dec. 29/30 and gives Jan. 3 capture/removal. PX-3 provides context but includes U.S. counternarcotics framing and a quote that after Maduro's removal repressive regime elements remained in control and Venezuela was hard to control territorially. Native investigation: opened CSIS imagery analysis titled 'Imagery from Venezuela Shows a Surgical Strike, Not Shock and Awe' at https://www.csis.org/analysis/imagery-venezuela-shows-surgical-strike-not-shock-and-awe. It limits scope/objective: law-enforcement mission, laser-focused capture, not shock-and-awe to bring entire Venezuelan security apparatus to heel, limited sites, corridor for helicopters, many air defense sites untouched, regular forces/headquarters largely not struck, no broad infrastructure attacks. Submitted CSIS relevant extract as defendant evidence ev_383c84572ac8_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-383c84572ac8; sha256 383c84572ac82a3ecd9381ccdb0ec103166ccc794197e35ccc8eec69088e7f17; 4294 bytes. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md. Planned filing: concede record proves force/location/capture, argue plaintiff still fails or at least has not proven the distinct element that U.S. commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over territory, as opposed to temporary tactical control incidental to a raid/extraction/law-enforcement mission. Offer CSIS evidence as DX-1 and use plaintiff evidence against plaintiff where helpful.

Plaintiff rebuttal work notes, arb-ex05-20260630003505.

Opportunity: rebuttals:plaintiff. Final actions allowed: submit_rebuttal or pass_phase_opportunity. Text limit 4000; remaining submitted evidence 5; remaining exhibits 9; remaining reports 3.

Record/evidence scan: Defendant submitted argument and one evidence item, DX-1: ev_383c84572ac8_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-383c84572ac8, CSIS imagery analysis. I used stat_evidence and read_evidence_range because exact content mattered to rebuttal.

DX-1 metadata: title "CSIS imagery analysis: surgical strike, not shock and awe"; source CSIS; published Jan. 9, 2026; retrieved 2026-06-30T00:55:30Z; sha256 383c84572ac82a3ecd9381ccdb0ec103166ccc794197e35ccc8eec69088e7f17; size 4294 bytes; text/plain.

DX-1 exact-content points: CSIS says more than 200 U.S. special operations forces surged through Caracas to capture Nicolas Maduro and Cilia Flores. It estimates military activity and battle damage at four sites: Fort Tiuna Military Complex, La Carlota Air Base, La Guaira Port, and El Higuerote Airport. It says U.S. forces attacked air defense batteries and radars to create a corridor for helicopters to enter Venezuelan territory and reach the target without interference. It says the operation was laser-focused on capture, not shock-and-awe against the entire Venezuelan security apparatus. It says Fort Tiuna damage was tightly focused and suggests an effort to isolate and neutralize specific capabilities tied directly to rapid response and regime protection. At La Guaira, damage was localized because of an air-defense unit; main docks and naval ships were not targeted. At Higuerote, imagery showed precision strike signs and no broader airfield strikes.

Rebuttal theory: Do not deny that DX-1 limits broad occupation. Instead, use it to prove plaintiff's narrower proposition: more than 200 SOF through Caracas, four Venezuelan sites, suppression of air defenses, a helicopter corridor into Venezuelan territory, and targeted neutralization of regime-protection capabilities are deliberate operational control over portions of Venezuela. The proposition says any portion and does not require permanent occupation, nationwide control, or shock-and-awe. Defense imports a take-and-hold requirement not in the text. A narrow military objective can still require establishing control over the targeted portion and corridor.

No new evidence submitted because the strongest rebuttal uses existing record evidence including defendant's own DX-1. Private journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.

Surrebuttal opportunity surrebuttals:defendant. Checked get_case and list_evidence. No new rebuttal evidence was submitted; evidence remains PX-1, PX-2, PX-3, and DX-1. Plaintiff rebuttal's strongest point is that 'any portion' includes target site, helicopter corridor, access route, and other temporary operational areas, and that DX-1 helps plaintiff because it describes SOF movement through Caracas, damage at multiple sites, and air-defense suppression to create a corridor. Planned response: answer directly that 'any portion' does not erase 'intended to establish control.' DX-1's corridor and limited-strike details show means of movement and force protection for a capture/extraction, not territorial control as the object of an offensive. Plaintiff's rule would convert any armed raid that secures a route or target into an invasion. No additional source needed because rebuttal raised a legal/inferential point from already admitted evidence. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.

Plaintiff closing work notes, arb-ex05-20260630003505.

Opportunity: closings:plaintiff. Only final action allowed is deliver_closing_statement; no evidence submission or technical reports allowed. Text limit 5000.

Required record/evidence scan: get_case and list_evidence called at start of closing. Evidence list unchanged: plaintiff PX-1 ev_e0e0fb921ec9..., PX-2 ev_7d30fcd0d9f7..., PX-3 ev_051e811bed13..., and defendant DX-1 ev_383c84572ac8.... No new case-packet files or changed metadata discovered. DX-1 was already read during rebuttal; no further exact byte read needed for closing.

Closing synthesis: The defense's best point is that the operation was surgical and capture-focused, not a broad shock-and-awe occupation. Concede that, because it is supported by DX-1 and does not defeat the proposition. The proposition requires an offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuela, not permanent or nationwide occupation. The record consensus is stronger than defendant admits: PX-1 official source establishes U.S. armed forces in Caracas, fortified military fortress, special operations, 150+ aircraft cover, ground extraction force; PX-2 independently establishes large-scale strike across Caracas and removal of Maduro/Flores; PX-3 establishes formal campaign context and scale; DX-1 confirms 200+ SOF through Caracas, battle damage/activity at four Venezuelan sites, and air-defense/radar attacks to create a helicopter corridor into Venezuelan territory. This is sufficient under preponderance.

Main response to surrebuttal: Defendant says control was a means, not an object. But the proposition says intended to establish control, not intended to keep control as the ultimate political end. If U.S. forces deliberately created a controlled helicopter corridor, neutralized rapid-response/regime-protection capabilities, entered/struck named Venezuelan sites, and seized a fortified objective to remove the head of government, the intended control over those portions is established even if temporary. If permanent holding were required, the phrase 'any portion' would do little work.

Private journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md. Filing will use only existing record evidence and no new sources.

Closing opportunity closings:defendant. Performed final get_case and list_evidence scan. No new evidence added during closing; record evidence remains four text extracts: PX-1 official Department of War account, PX-2 PBS/AP timeline, PX-3 CFR explainer, DX-1 CSIS imagery analysis. Plaintiff closing concedes no broad occupation/governance but argues intent to control is built into planned suppression/corridor/target dominance. Defense closing plan: concede record proves U.S. military force in Caracas and capture/removal of Maduro/Flores. Press burden and source-consensus gap: proposition requires intent to establish territorial control, not merely intentional force or temporary operational conditions. Plaintiff's theory converts any successful armed raid into invasion because any raid secures routes/doors/landing zones. Sources converge on capture/extraction; they do not converge on territorial control as object. PX-1 objective is bringing Maduro to justice/joint military-law-enforcement; DX-1 describes law-enforcement/capture focus, no shock-and-awe, limited sites, many defenses untouched, and existing structures left in place. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.
